title: DREAM MACHINE | Creative AI News & Insight { OCT 25 -Issue 4 }
text:
DREAM MACHINE | Creative AI News & Insight { OCT 25 -Issue 4 }
Report this article
Pete Woodbridge
Pete Woodbridge
Published Oct 23, 2025
+ Follow
Welcome to the fourth edition of DREAM MACHINE. Tracking news, insight and developments on AI in the Creative Industries: covering Film, Music, Games, Media, Advertising & Entertainment. The good, the bad and the ugly.
Plus news and tutorials from all the latest tools and models. Made for creators, developers, dreamers, artists and content peeps who want to stay up to date with all of the possibilities, contradictions & debates.
Created and curated by
Pete Woodbridge
Dr John O'Hare
Steve Moyler
& the
dreamlab
community.
Editors Pick
Is the Internet Dead Yet?
“Only one in five site visits are now from real humans.” —
Cloudflare / Imperva, 2025
The Internet we once knew — a messy, human-driven web of creativity, curiosity, and connection — is quietly being replaced by something else. According to new data from
Graphite (2025)
, more than
half of all new online content
is already generated by AI. The surge began after the release of large language models in 2022, accelerating through 2024 before stabilizing at a 50–50 human–machine split. But while the machines are writing, they’re also reading — and increasingly, they’re reading only each other.
Machine Traffic, Machine Readers
51%
of global web traffic now comes from bots.
79%
of all bot activity is for AI training.
Cloudflare / Fastly / Imperva, 2025
Reports from major infrastructure providers show that bots have now overtaken people as the Internet’s main inhabitants. Cloudflare, Fastly, and Imperva all confirm that
automated systems generate over half of all web traffic
, with just
one in five visits
made by real humans. AI crawlers like
GPTBot, ClaudeBot, and Meta’s scrapers
dominate, endlessly trawling for data to feed new models. The result: a recursive, self-consuming loop of AI systems reading, rewriting, and re-indexing each other’s output — a synthetic mirror world made by and for machines.
A Synthetic Economy
The generative content market is projected to hit
$80 B by 2030
.
Grand View Research, 2025
Gartner and Europol forecast that
90–99% of online content
could be AI-generated or AI-assisted by 2030. The
$14.8 billion
generative-AI content market (2024) is projected to quintuple within five years, driven by automation in marketing, entertainment, and publishing. Yet as AI consumes the Internet, researchers warn of
“synthetic contamination”
— models trained on their own synthetic outputs, losing touch with real human language, knowledge, and culture.
The Dead Internet — or Just a Different One?
If the early Internet was built on
human attention
, today’s version runs on
machine attention
— bots crawling bots, LLMs summarising LLMs, AI systems endlessly feeding each other’s loops. The “
dead Internet
” isn’t a conspiracy theory; it’s the logical endpoint of efficiency, automation, and scale. Humans are still here trying to be heard, but we’re no longer the main audience.
What Survives the Shift
In this new ecology,
authenticity and provenance
become the new scarcity. When everything online can be faked, cloned, or generated at near zero cost,
proof that someone real made something
becomes the most valuable signal of all
.
The Internet isn’t quite dead, — but the human web inside it might be changing drastically as we know it. This is an interesting
experiment from dutch researchers
who built a social platform stripped to bare essentials using AI agents. The bots fractured into warring tribes within hours. A narrow elite captured all attention and extremist echo chambers flourished. Maybe it's the architecture thats the problem?
Or maybe its time to start a new one?
